scope: All youth age 18 and under on regular Muni service; universal eligibility regardless of income or residency
funding_mechanism: City/SFMTA operating support within existing fare-policy framework
outcomes: Established a real local precedent for universal free-fare access within a major rider group and simplified boarding for youth.
key_lessons: Most transferable precedent because it already operates inside the same agency and political system. Shows universality is politically possible for at least one group.

policy_name: Free Muni for low- and moderate-income seniors
scope: Means-tested free access for SF seniors 65+ using Clipper
funding_mechanism: City/SFMTA operating support targeted to eligible residents
outcomes: Demonstrates San Francisco already uses targeted free-fare policy for another high-need group.
key_lessons: Targeted design is fiscally easier to defend than universal adult eligibility, but preserves enrollment and eligibility administration.

scope: Citywide bus system fare-free for several years; streetcar remains fare-free
funding_mechanism: Local support plus temporary/unstable funding including pandemic-era support
outcomes: Showed zero-fare implementation at city scale, but later fare restoration on buses highlighted vulnerability when long-term recurring funding is not locked in.
key_lessons: Use Kansas City to argue that fare-free transit is operationally possible, but also to warn that unstable funding can unravel a celebrated program.

scope: Fare-free transit for registered city residents
funding_mechanism: Municipal funding tied to residency and local tax base
outcomes: Helped arrest ridership decline and improved access, but evidence suggests limited car-to-transit mode shift.
key_lessons: Good precedent for access framing; weaker as proof of major congestion reduction. Residency-linked design is not directly transferable to SF's intra-city equity goals.

scope: National fare-free transit at point of use
outcomes: Established the highest-profile modern free-transit example, but under national political and fiscal conditions unlike San Francisco's.
key_lessons: Useful for proving that free transit is administratively possible at scale, but weak as a direct fiscal or governance analogue for SFMTA.

scope: Regional bus service kept fare-free with grant/state support
funding_mechanism: Grant-backed and state-supported funding with ongoing fundraising/community support
outcomes: Zero-fare service became a strong equity narrative, but continued support depends on ongoing external funding rather than an obviously permanent local revenue stream.
key_lessons: Best precedent for pilot framing and equity messaging; weaker as evidence of a permanent self-financing model for a system like Muni.
